elements.sent-2411-001: The receiver-function (RF) technique aims to recover receiver-side crustal and mantle structures by deconvolving either the radial or transverse component with the vertical component seismogram.
elements.sent-2411-002: Analysis of the variations of RFs along backazimuth and slowness is the key in determining the geometry and anisotropic properties of the crustal layers.
elements.sent-2411-003: Nonetheless, pseudorandom nuisance effects, influenced by the unknown earthquake source signature and seismic noise, are produced by the deconvolution process and obstruct precise comparisons of RFs across different backazimuths.
elements.sent-2411-004: Various methods such as weighted stacking, sparsity-induced transform and supervised denoising neural-network have been developed to reduce the nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, the common assumption of the nuisance effects as random Gaussian proves inadequate.
elements.sent-2411-006: Supervised denoising neural-network struggles to generalize effectively in intricate tectonic environments like subduction zones.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this study, we take an unsupervised approach where a network-based representation of a group of RFs with similar raypaths, enables disentanglement of the coherent crustal effects from the RF-specific nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-008: The representation learning task is performed using symmetric autoencoders (SymAE).
elements.sent-2411-009: We show that SymAE successfully produces virtual RFs that predominantly capture coherent crustal effects from the RFs within the group, while reducing unwanted nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our method was applied to synthetic RFs that were contaminated with nuisance effects, which are derived from the real data.
elements.sent-2411-011: The generated virtual RFs (both radial and transverse) in this case exhibit superior quality and accurately match the true synthetic RFs --- our method outperforms bin-wise linear stacking and phase-weighted stacking methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: When employed with real data from the Cascadia Subduction Zone, our approach produced enhanced radial and transverse RFs.
elements.sent-2411-013: Additionally, it facilitated a clear interpretation of a dual-layer subducting slab.
elements.sent-2411-014: We also provided sanity checks to verify the accuracy of the network-derived virtual RFs.
elements.sent-2411-015: One major advantage of our method is its ability to utilize all available earthquakes, irrespective of their signal quality, thereby enhancing reproducibility and enabling automation in RF analysis.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, this advantage allows us to utilize temporary stations which measure only a few earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-017: Receiver functions (RFs), computed using several teleseismic earthquakes, provide a powerful method for investigating crustal and upper mantle structures below a particular receiver.
elements.sent-2411-018: This study focuses on analyzing the changes in the RF relative to the backazimuth and the distance to the teleseismic earthquake, which are essential for accurate subsurface characterization.
elements.sent-2411-019: Careful analysis of these variations and comparison with forward modeling results~\citep{levin2008seismic, Savage2007, liu2015crustal can reveal important information about the orientation and magnitude of Earth's anisotropy, as well as the 3-D geometry of subsurface features, providing researchers with valuable insights into the tectonic evolution and dynamics of a given region.}

elements.sent-2411-026: To be precise, nuisance effects are unwanted signals in RFs that arise through two distinct pathways during the deconvolution of the radial with the vertical component of the seismogram.

elements.subsection-2411-004: Multiple Stations and Datapoints for Training

elements.sent-2411-136: Each datapoint collects all the RFs associated with a specific bin, specifically the $j$-th bin, which is characterized by backazimuth and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-137: The total number of training datapoints corresponds to the number of available bins.
elements.sent-2411-138: For an individual receiver, this number is influenced by the number of earthquakes it records and their geographical distribution.
elements.sent-2411-139: In our research, we train using datapoints from multiple stations simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-140: Thus, the total number of datapoints is the aggregate of all bins across these receivers.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our main objective is to detect variations in coherent crustal information between different datapoints, despite the presence of varying nuisance effects.

elements.sent-2411-146: In this part, we focus on the task of learning representations of RFs using a symmetric autoencoder~\citep[SymAE]{Bharadwaj2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Representation learning can be conceptualized as a method that encompasses both forward and inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-148: Inverse problem (inference to obtain a latent code): The process of extracting meaningful features or representations from the input RF data can be seen to solve an inverse problem.
elements.sent-2411-149: In this scenario, for a datapoint $\mathbf{r _j$, the objective is to identify a latent code $\mathbf{H _j$ that captures both the crustal effects and the nuisance effects that contribute to the formation of $\mathbf{r _j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: This step is achieved through an encoder network $\enc$ to produce the code \begin{eqnarray}\mathbf{H}_j = \enc(\mathbf{r}_j),\end{eqnarray} where the encoder maps the input data to a latent space.
elements.sent-2411-151: Solving this inverse problem is crucial because it transforms the RFs into a compact, informative representation that facilitates further processing.
elements.sent-2411-152: Forward problem (reconstruction of RFs): The second phase involves solving a forward problem, where the task is to reconstruct the original RFs from the latent code $\mathbf{H _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-153: This step is performed using a decoder network $\dec$, which generates the output RFs \begin{eqnarray}\hat{\mathbf{r}}_j = \dec(\mathbf{H}_j).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-154: The decoder aims to transform the latent code back into a space similar to the original data.
elements.sent-2411-155: The encoder and decoder networks are optimized by reducing the reconstruction loss \begin{eqnarray}\label{eqn:optimize} J = \sum_j \sum_i \|\mathbf{r}_j[i] - \hat{\mathbf{r}}_j[i]\|^2_2,\quad \text{where}\quad \hat{\mathbf{r}}_j = \dec(\enc(\mathbf{r}_j)).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-157: Reducing $J$ leads the model to develop an effective latent representation.
elements.sent-2411-158: Essentially, the encoder compresses the information efficiently, while the decoder evaluates how well and accurately the latent representation reflects the original data.
elements.sent-2411-159: In training, the goal of achieving a reconstruction with a reduced $J$ suggests that $\mathbf{H _j$ probably preserves the crucial features of $\mathbf{r _j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Unlike traditional autoencoders ~\citep{Hinton2006,ng2011sparse,vincent2008extracting , the SymAE encoder network is uniquely designed to solve two distinct inverse problems, producing two separate latent codes.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The architecture of SymAE is highlighted in Fig. ~\ref{fig:network .}
elements.sent-2411-162: The first inverse problem involves estimating the crustal effects shared among the RFs of $\mathbf{r _j$, while the second focuses on estimating the nuisance effects specific to each RF element within $\mathbf{r _j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: The entire latent code $\mathbf{H _j$ is constructed by combining the results from these two separate inverse problems.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Upon estimating the crustal and nuisance effects for each datapoint, during the decoding stage (forward modeling), we can opt to apply these effects across various datapoints --- this capability allows us to create virtual RFs that were not originally recorded.
elements.sent-2411-165: In essence, the dual-inverse problem approach facilitates forward modeling by allowing nuisance effects to be transferred from one RF to another.
elements.sent-2411-166: In summary, our results indicate that once the training of the encoder and decoder networks within SymAE is complete, they can generate virtual RFs with a better signal-to-noise ratio.
elements.sent-2411-167: This improvement enhances the crustal effects while minimizing the influence of nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-168: Before delving into this topic, let us first explore the architectural aspects of SymAE.
elements.sent-2411-169: SymAE consists of two distinct encoders: a symmetric encoder ($\encp$) and a nuisance encoder ($\encn$), which transform the RFs in $\rec$ into coherent and nuisance latent codes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-170: The details of these encoders are described below.
elements.sent-2411-171: The $\encp$ is a deep neural network designed to identify coherence or similarity among RFs within $\rec$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Essentially, the architecture of $\encp$ ensures that, when applied to $\rec$, it generates a latent code $\coh_j \in \mathbb{R ^p$ that represents only coherent crustal effects, excluding nuisance effects.}
elements.sent-2411-173: According to the universal approximation theorem~\cite{hornik1989multilayer, cybenko1989approximation , an unconstrained $\encp$ would capture both coherent crustal effects and nuisance effects.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Consequently, $\encp$ is subject to a permutation invariance constraint, ensuring that the order of the RFs in $\rec$ does not affect the output of the network $\encp$.
elements.sent-2411-175: Mathematically, this means that \begin{eqnarray}\coh_j = \encp(\rec) = \encp(\Pi(\rec)). \label{eqn:Ccode}\end{eqnarray} Here, $\Pi$ is a random permutation function that reorders the elements of $\rec$ according to a random permutation $\Pi$ of the indices $\{1, 2, \ldots, n_j\}$, i.e., \begin{eqnarray}\Pi(\mathbf{r}_j) = \left[\mathbf{r}_{j}[\Pi(1)], \mathbf{r}_j[\Pi(2)], \ldots, \mathbf{r}_j[\Pi(n)]\right],\end{eqnarray} where $\Pi$ is a bijection $\Pi: \{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ that shuffles the indices of $\rec$ randomly.
elements.sent-2411-176: The central concept is to consider $\encp$ as addressing the inverse problem of inferring crustal effects from RFs derived from various nearby earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-177: The solution to this inverse problem remains unchanged regardless of the sequence in which the earthquakes are recorded by the station, illustrating permutation invariance.
elements.sent-2411-178: In essence, maintaining the permutation invariance in $\encp$ promotes the inference of crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-179: Alternatively, by imposing permutation invariance, we ensure that nuisance effects, which differ among RFs, cannot be accurately inferred by $\encp$.
elements.sent-2411-180: The above constraint implies that the function $\encp(\mathbf{r _j)$ maintains symmetry in terms of the sequence of RFs within $\mathbf{r _j$, thus we refer to it as a symmetric encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: In practice, we adopt the network architectures outlined in~\cite{zaheer2017deepsets that can approximate functions with permutation-invariance properties.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Specifically, we employ the mean operation over RFs as the permutation-invariance mechanism after applying a neural network $\encpone$ on each element of $\mathbf{r _j$, resulting in the coherent code as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\coh_{j} = \encp(\mathbf{r}_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_j}(\encpone(\mathbf{r}_j[i])).\end{eqnarray}.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The mean operation maintains symmetry with regard to the sequence of RFs, thus ensuring the required permutation invariance for $\encp$, as described in Eq. ~\ref{eqn:Ccode .}
elements.sent-2411-184: This architectural constraint applied to $\encp$ is demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:network .}
elements.sent-2411-185: In this figure, solid arrows represent the propagation of coherent crustal information across the SymAE network.
elements.sent-2411-186: SymAE is designed to reconstruct the input datapoint without loss of information, requiring its encoder to infer both the coherent crustal effects and the nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-187: Since the coherent encoder cannot infer nuisance effects, SymAE uses another encoder, called the nuisance encoder $\encn$, to estimate these effects.
elements.sent-2411-188: Two features define $\encn$ : 1) it lacks the architectural constraints (i.e. symmetry) found in $\encp$, being instead constrained during training through stochastic regularization~\citep{wang2013fast,kingma2015variational ; 2) the network operates on each element of the datapoint $\mathbf{r _j$, similar to performing independent inferences on each RF to estimate the respective nuisance effects.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The nuisance influences within each RF are represented by the code $\nuis_{j ^{(i) \in \mathbb{R ^m$, defined as follows: \begin{eqnarray}\nuis_j^{(i)} = \mathfrak{N}(\encn(\rec[i])).\end{eqnarray}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: In this context, the function $\mathfrak{N}$ introduces random perturbations to $\mathbf{r [i]$ throughout the training process, serving as a form of stochastic regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-191: This can be interpreted as introducing noise to the estimates of $\encn$.
elements.sent-2411-192: Essentially, we are indicating to the decoder that the inference of $\encn$ carries some uncertainty, thereby compelling the decoder to place greater reliance on the estimates from the symmetric encoder.
elements.sent-2411-193: In practice, $\mathfrak{N}$ is implemented using Bernoulli dropout~\citep{srivastava2014dropout , as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:network , throughout training to introduce high probability random perturbations into the nuisance code.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: It is important to observe that, unlike the traditional use of dropouts for regularizing functions approximated by deep neural networks, SymAE employs dropouts to avoid the nuisance encoder unintentionally capturing coherent crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-195: The encoders are responsible for inferring both crustal and nuisance effects, generating latent codes denoted $\coh_j$ and $\nuis^{(i) _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: The decoder is tasked with utilizing these latent codes to perform forward modeling, thereby reconstructing the RFs within each datapoint.
elements.sent-2411-197: To achieve this, the decoder network is trained to create each element of the output datapoint $\hat{\mathbf{r _j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: This network takes as input a concatenated latent code, formed from latent codes produced by both symmetric and nuisance encoders.
elements.sent-2411-199: For the $i$-th element of the $j$-th datapoint, we represent the concatenated latent code as \begin{eqnarray}\lc^{(i)}_j=[\coh_j; \nuis^{(i)}_j].\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-200: Subsequently, this concatenated latent code is inputted to a deep neural network $\dec$, facilitating the generation of a RF: \begin{eqnarray}\hat{\mathbf{r}}^{(i)}_j = \dec(\lc^{(i)}_j) \label{eqn:recont}\end{eqnarray} This is demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:network .}
elements.sent-2411-201: Finally, output datapoint is formed as a vector of all the generated RFs \begin{eqnarray}\hat{\mathbf{r}}_j=[\hat{\mathbf{r}}_j^{(1)}, \hat{\mathbf{r}}_j^{(2)}, \ldots, \hat{\mathbf{r}}_j^{(n_j)}].\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-202: In summary, SymAE effectively reconstructs the data without much loss utilizing two types of encoder: coherent and nuisance encoders.
elements.sent-2411-203: These encoders have their individual strengths and weaknesses, but work together in a complementary manner to accurately reconstruct the original RFs.
elements.sent-2411-204: The model involves three neural networks, denoted $\encp$, $\encn$, and $\dec$, which are trained simultaneously to minimize the reconstruction loss specified in Eq. ~\ref{eqn:optimize .}
elements.sent-2411-205: We used neural networks similar to Alex-net~\citep{krizhevsky2012imagenet , integrating convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers to define encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-206: In selecting the hyperparameters for SymAE, such as the latent code length, the dropout rate during training, and additional parameters related to the convolutional layers in both the encoder and decoder, we conducted synthetic experiments.

elements.sent-2411-223: To effectively remove common nuisance effects within a set of RFs, we employ the technique of mean-subtraction.
elements.sent-2411-224: This process helps clarify the interpretation of virtual RFs by highlighting differences in crustal effects while reducing the impact of nuisance effects.

elements.sent-2411-233: Two velocity models were employed for the synthetic experiment.
elements.sent-2411-234: This model is characterized by a crust with $10\%$ anisotropy, positioned above an isotropic mantle.
elements.sent-2411-235: The anisotropic layer has the fast axis oriented at an azimuth angle of $0^{\circ}$N.
elements.sent-2411-236: The layers dipped $20^{\circ}$ from the horizontal with a strike of $0^{\circ}$N.
elements.sent-2411-237: This model represents a subduction zone that features two dipping layers beneath a crust that exhibits transverse anisotropy.
elements.sent-2411-238: Anisotropy in both models leads to systematic variations in PS-wave propagation along the backazimuth, whereas the interface dip leads to variations with epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-239: Following the RF calculation, we demonstrate how the presence of nuisance effects complicates the interpretation of layer geometry and anisotropy when attempting to extract the aforementioned variations.
elements.sent-2411-240: The detailed steps to generate synthetic RFs for the models described above are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-242: At each station, the crustal impulse responses for the vertical (Z), radial (R), and transverse (T) components were calculated using the PyRaysum software ~\citep{Bloch_Audet_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-243: The backazimuth and epicentral distance were binned with a $10^{\circ}$ interval.
elements.sent-2411-244: At each bin, crustal impulse responses were generated for randomly selected backazimuth and epicentral distance within the bin's range.
elements.sent-2411-245: The count of impulse responses assigned to each bin was randomly set, varying between 15 and 30.
elements.sent-2411-246: This simulates the scenario where certain bins experience more earthquakes than others.
elements.sent-2411-248: Each crustal impulse response was convolved with a distinct real seismogram, trimmed 20\,s before and after the P-wave arrival, to create synthetic seismograms.
elements.sent-2411-249: This method replicates the variations in bandwidth and zeros seen in the source spectrum commonly observed in actual earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-250: Each real seismogram was uniquely selected without replacement from a large dataset, and the resulting synthetic seismograms were bandpass filtered between 0.005 and 1 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-251: Random Gaussian noise, bandpass filtered within the same frequency range as the synthetic seismograms, was added to all three components independently.
elements.sent-2411-252: The noise level was adjusted to maintain a specified signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), which was randomly selected from a log-normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-253: The SNR was determined by comparing the root-mean-square (rms) amplitude in a 10\,s interval after the arrival of the P wave to the rms amplitude in a 10\,s interval before the arrival of the P wave, both measured on the vertical component of the seismogram.
elements.sent-2411-255: Frequency domain deconvolution was employed to generate synthetic radial and transverse receiver functions.
elements.sent-2411-256: The additive noise in the seismograms was capped at a maximum SNR of 2.
elements.sent-2411-257: The deconvolution process was regularized using a water level parameter of 0.01 and a Gaussian filter with a width of 5.
elements.sent-2411-258: The resulting receiver functions (RFs) were normalized with respect to the P wave at $t=0$.
elements.sent-2411-260: We created datapoints by dividing the $0^{\circ}$--${360 ^{\circ}$ backazimuth into $\text{10 ^{\circ}$ intervals and dividing the $40^{\circ}$--$100^{\circ}$ epicentral distance also into $10^{\circ}$ intervals.}}
elements.sent-2411-261: This procedure results in $36\times6\times6$ datapoints per model, corresponding to the number of azimuth bins, epicentral distance bins, and receivers.
elements.sent-2411-262: In addition to the RFs with nuisance effects described above, true radial and transverse RFs were created using the same procedure.
elements.sent-2411-263: This involved using a Gaussian pulse as the source wavelet and excluding any additive noise.
elements.sent-2411-264: These true RFs are crucial for assessing performance, although they are not needed for training.
elements.sent-2411-265: For both synthetic Earth models, each datapoint was divided so that 80\% of the RFs were used for training, while the remaining 20\% were reserved for testing.
elements.sent-2411-266: The datapoints for each of the Earth models were processed after two separate SymAE models were initialized independently.
elements.sent-2411-267: In addition, datapoints for the radial and transverse components were trained independently.
elements.sent-2411-268: The number of convolutional layers in the encoders was determined on the basis of the learning curve, ensuring an optimal balance between model complexity and overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-269: The coherent code length ($p$) and the nuisance code length ($m$) were fixed at 200 and 80 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-270: Early stopping was implemented with a maximum of 30 epochs and a learning rate of 0.0004 was employed.
elements.sent-2411-271: The optimal dropout rate at the output of the nuisance encoder was fixed to 0.3.
elements.sent-2411-272: After training the network on the datasets, we computed a virtual RF without nuisance effects for each datapoint at all stations, as described in Sec.. ~\ref{sec:virRF .}

elements.sent-2411-295: RF studies have been widely utilized to characterize crustal structures in subduction zones~\citep{Cassidy1995,Bloch2023,Savage2007,ShiomiK .}
elements.sent-2411-296: However, the complex structural settings in the subduction zones of ocean plates, which include low-velocity zones, partial melting, and accretionary prisms, due to geodynamic processes, present significant obstacles in the extraction of crustal structures from RFs.
elements.sent-2411-297: The complex scattering of different seismic phases poses significant challenges for interpreting RFs, primarily due to the presence of nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-298: Conventional methods for the removal of nuisance effects, such as stacking and supervised denoising, are inefficient for complex crustal structures in subduction zones.
elements.sent-2411-299: The Cascadia Subduction Zone, in particular, has been the focus of extensive study due to its intricate slab morphology, link to seismic hazards, and geodynamic processes ~\citep{Langston1979,Nicholson2005,Bloch2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-300: To reduce nuisance effects, ~\cite{Bloch2023 and ~\cite{Audet2009Seismic filtered noisy seismograms based on the signal-to-noise ratio and linearly stacked RFs within the bin.}}

elements.sent-2411-367: It means that converted seismic waves, originating from the crustal structures due to distant earthquakes within a bin, have comparable raypaths and minimal travel-time differences.

elements.sent-2411-373: Receiver functions (RFs) are an essential technique for analyzing crust and mantle structures beneath seismic stations; however, they are often contaminated with pseudorandom nuisance effects that reduce the precision and reliability of RF measurements.
elements.sent-2411-374: We introduce an innovative approach to removing these unwanted nuisance effects from RFs through an unsupervised deep learning framework using symmetric autoencoders.
elements.sent-2411-375: This method effectively disentangled the nuisance effects from crustal effects, allowing the generation of virtual RFs with similar nuisance effects, and averaged them out to remove the common nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-376: Our approach outperforms linear stacking and phase-weighted stacking in denoising synthetic RFs, which are affected by realistic non-Gaussian nuisance effects, resulting in high-quality and consistent RFs.
elements.sent-2411-377: Unlike stacked RFs, the polarity reversal in transverse RFs along the backazimuth is clearly depicted in virtual RFs.
elements.sent-2411-378: The application of our method in RFs from seismic stations at the Cascadia Subduction Zone's forearc has shown strong agreement with previous studies.
elements.sent-2411-379: Our method better delineated the various layers of the subducting slab and enhanced the crustal effects, which were not as clear in linearly stacked RFs.
elements.sent-2411-380: Virtual RFs demonstrated consistency across backazimuth, epicentral distances, and nearby stations.
elements.sent-2411-381: The method was proved robust by integrating all available RFs, regardless of their signal quality.
elements.sent-2411-382: The automated and human-free process of our method provides both scalability and reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-383: The compelling results of this study suggest that our approach can greatly improve RF analysis by eliminating nuisances, enabling more precise interpretations of crustal and mantle structures, and revealing new insights previously obscured by noisy RFs, thereby advancing teleseismic imaging and solid-Earth studies.

elements.sent-2411-421: Analysis of the variations of RFs along the backazimuth and slowness is the key in determining the geometry and anisotropic properties of the crustal structures.
elements.sent-2411-422: However, the deconvolution introduces pseudorandom nuisance effects, due to unknown earthquake source signatures and seismic noise, which obstruct the precise extraction of backazimuth and slowness dependent crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-423: Our goal is to obtain RFs with minimal nuisance effects, while preserving the crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-424: In this study, we introduced a new method for reducing nuisance effects in RFs.
elements.sent-2411-425: This method generates virtual RFs through a deep generative model, namely symmetric variational autoencoders (SymVAE).
elements.sent-2411-426: Our autoencoder efficiently learns to disentangle coherent crustal effects and nuisance effects within its latent space, given a set of RFs derived from a cluster of nearby earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-427: This disentanglement enables generation of virtual RFs which exhibits minimal nuisance effects while preserving the coherent crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-428: We tested SymVAE using synthetic RFs with ambient seismic noise.
elements.sent-2411-429: We also tested using dense seismic networks in two distinct geological settings: the Cascadia subduction zone and southern California.
elements.sent-2411-430: We compared our method with linear and phase-weighted averaging.
elements.sent-2411-431: In both synthetic and real RFs, the generated virtual RFs demonstrate enhanced information related to crustal structures.
elements.sent-2411-432: We have also quantitatively assessed the performance.
elements.sent-2411-433: One major advantage of our method over traditional methods is its ability to utilize all available earthquake data, regardless of signal quality, resulting in improved backazimuth and slowness coverage.
elements.sent-2411-434: To illustrate this emphasis, we consider the systematic variation of the PS phase (forward-scattered S waves from the Moho caused by an incident P wave) with respect to backazimuth and distance.
elements.sent-2411-435: Numerous studies have extracted the fast-polarization direction and magnitude of crustal anisotropy from the arrival of PS within RFs~\citep{McNamara1993, Bianchi2010, Zheng2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-436: Similarly, backazimuth variations of the PS arrival in both radial and transverse RFs have been employed to calculate the dip azimuth, dip angle, and depth of subducting slabs~\citep{ShiomiK .}
elements.sent-2411-437: To analyze RF variations with respect to backazimuth and epicentral distance, one must be able to compare RFs from multiple earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-438: The nuisance effects are unique to each earthquake and it should be emphasized that the common assumption that these effects can be modeled as random Gaussian noise frequently proves inadequate~\citep{kolb2014receiver,Bodin2014Inversion,bona1998variance .}
elements.sent-2411-439: This means that insufficient regularization in deconvolution amplifies the seismic noise linked to the spectral zeros of the source signature, which causes nuisance effects~\citep{Akuhara2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-440: Conversely, when regularizing the deconvolution, nuisance effects appear as biases that are specific to the regularization method.
elements.sent-2411-441: Secondly, deconvolution, which is associated with cross-correlation, results in crosstalk between the scattered waves from crustal structures and seismic noise in the vertical and radial components.
elements.sent-2411-442: Crosstalk increases interference and makes the interpretation of converted seismic phases related to crustal structures (referred to as crustal effects) more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-443: Various researchers~\citep{Park2000,Zhang2022,zhang2024crustal have recognized that pseudo-random nuisance effects make it difficult to interpret receiver functions, highlighting the need to address these effects to achieve the accurate extraction of crustal effects.}
elements.sent-2411-444: A strategy to improve the quality of RFs involves careful selection and identification of usable earthquakes through automated processes~\citep{Crotwell2005,Yang2016 and machine learning frameworks~\cite{Gong2022,Krueger2021,sabermahani2024deepRFQC .}}
elements.sent-2411-445: However, removing earthquakes will decrease azimuthal coverage.
elements.sent-2411-446: Therefore, most of the methods focus on reducing nuisance effects\footnote{The nuisance effects cannot be effectively removed through standard bandpass filtering techniques, as they share a frequency band similar to that of crustal converted phases.. in RFs.}
elements.sent-2411-447: In the literature, these methods can be classified into two categories.
elements.sent-2411-448: The first category corresponds to data-driven methods, where multiple RFs are averaged to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).. Examples include.
elements.sent-2411-449: The methods in this category are efficient when dealing with 1) permanent stations that record numerous earthquakes; 2) a uniform earthquake distribution across backazimuth and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-450: The averaging procedure in these methods assumes that nuisance effects follow a simple distribution, such as a Gaussian distribution, which is inaccurate.
elements.sent-2411-451: In practice, the earthquake distribution is non-uniform, meaning that some azimuthal or epicentral-distance bins have more earthquakes than others, which can lead to less efficient averaging for certain bins~\citep{Levin1997,Ozakin2015,Bloch2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-452: The selection of weights in weighted averaging methods can be subjective.
elements.sent-2411-453: Model-based techniques comprise the second category of methods that aim to minimize nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-454: To improve RF quality, these methods rely on assumptions about the Earth's velocity structure.
elements.sent-2411-455: They apply constraints to a gather of available RFs, ordered according to either backazimuth or epicentral distance.. Examples include.
elements.sent-2411-456: Methods in this category are unsuccessful when applied to intricate geological environments such as subduction zones, which are different from the assumed (simpler) models.
elements.sent-2411-457: CCP averaging may result in artifacts~\citep{Zheng2014 due to mismatches between the assumed velocity model and the Earth's true model.}
elements.sent-2411-458: An important drawback of supervised deep learning is the difficulty in assessing its performance on real seismic data, which originates from a distribution different from that assumed during training.
elements.sent-2411-459: This paper aims to train neural networks on RFs from various earthquakes and then synthesize new virtual RFs with minimal nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-460: We demonstrate that these virtual RFs retain crustal effects, enabling the extraction of their dependence on backazimuth and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-461: In computer vision, various approaches have been devised to generate new images by learning the distribution of the training images~\citep{Kingma2014,diffusion,Kobyzev_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-462: We extend the variational autoencoder (VAE) framework~\citep{Kingma2014,bishop2023deep , a nonlinear latent variable model in which the latent variables capture key features of the training data.}
elements.sent-2411-463: VAE can generate virtual data (such as images, audio, or seismograms) by first selecting samples in the low-dimensional latent space and then decoding these samples.
elements.sent-2411-464: In geophysics, autoencoders have been used to identify and classify seismograms based on a low-dimensional representation of the seismograms~\citep{Valentine2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-465: VAE has been used to generate realistic ground motions corresponding to specific physical features such as earthquake magnitude, soil type, and source focal mechanism~\citep{NingXie2024,Fayaz2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-466: The use of the generated virtual data is problem-specific, where in our case we are interested in generating RFs with minimal nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-467: In order to do so, we need to disentangle the nuisance effects from the crustal effects in the latent space --- traditional VAE architectures do not have this feature.
elements.sent-2411-468: Recent studies have focused on learning the disentangled latent space within the VAE framework~\citep{Mathieu2016,burgess2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-469: In this study, we employ a variation of VAE called symmetric variational autoencoders~\citep[SymVAE]{bharadwaj2024vsymae to learn a latent space where the crustal and nuisance effects are disentangled.}
elements.sent-2411-470: To validate our methodology, we applied it to RFs derived from synthetic seismograms with real earthquake source signatures and ambient seismic noise.
elements.sent-2411-471: We considered a crustal model with an anisotropic layer.
elements.sent-2411-472: The quality of virtual RFs was assessed by calculating the normalized correlation coefficient (NCC) with respect to the true RFs.
elements.sent-2411-473: Virtual RFs exhibited higher NCC compared to RFs obtained by linear and phase-weighted averaging.
elements.sent-2411-474: We have evaluated the effectiveness of our method in complex geological settings, such as the Cascadia subduction zone and southern California.
elements.sent-2411-475: Virtual radial RFs from Cascadia clearly highlight the scattered S-waves produced by the velocity interfaces of the subducting oceanic slab, as reported by~\cite{Bloch2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-476: Radial virtual RFs along a profile intersecting the San Andreas fault in southern California show sharp variations in converted S-wave delay time, indicating inhomogeneous crustal thickness.
elements.sent-2411-477: Finally, the virtual RFs showed spatial consistency with patterns similar to those of adjacent seismic stations, confirming their geological validity.
elements.sent-2411-478: In contrast to many previous studies that discard low SNR RFs, our method leverages all available earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-479: Our approach requires fewer assumptions regarding Earth models and nuisance statistics.
elements.sent-2411-480: It enables the generation of high-quality RFs to increase the coverage of the backazimuth and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-481: Furthermore, like other deep learning techniques, our method is automated and scalable to various datasets and geographic areas.
elements.sent-2411-482: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-483: We begin by detailing the grouping of RFs necessary for preparing a training dataset for our deep-generative model i.e. SymVAE.
elements.sent-2411-484: Next, we explain how SymVAE disentangles crustal effects from nuisance effects and generates nuisance-minimized RFs.
elements.sent-2411-485: We then detail synthetic experiments and compare SymVAE's performance with conventional averaging methods.
elements.sent-2411-486: Finally, our method is applied to real data to evaluate its performance in complex geological settings.
elements.section-2411-010: Grouping Receiver Functions To Create Datapoints
elements.sent-2411-487: A datapoint is an element from the input dataset employed to train a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-488: In this paper, each datapoint refers to a group of RFs with a similar backazimuth angle and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-489: For each datapoint, our method aims to extract the coherent crustal effects shared by the RFs as discussed below.
elements.sent-2411-490: The wavefield due to a teleseismic earthquake, incident on the crustal structure near a seismic station, can be approximated as a planewave characterized by a seismic wavelet $s$ and a propagation direction defined by the wavenumber vector $\p$.
elements.sent-2411-491: The interaction of this planewave with the crustal structure results in measured waveforms at the station, which can be expressed as a convolution of the seismic wavelet with the Earth's impulse response: \begin{equation}\dz(t,\p) = \int_{\tau}s(t-\tau)\gz(\tau,\p)\,\text{d}\tau+\nz(t), \label{eqn:one}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\dr(t,\p) = \int_{\tau}s(t-\tau)\gr(\tau,\p)\,\text{d}\tau+\mathit{\nr(t)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-492: Here, time is indicated as $t$, the vertical and radial components of the measured seismogram are indicated by using $\dz$ and $\dr$, respectively, while $\nz$ and $\nr$ represent uncorrelated noise in these components.
elements.sent-2411-493: The vertical and radial impulse responses of the crust beneath the seismic station, $\gz$ and $\gr$, are necessary for imaging~\citep{Tauzin2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-494: The seismic wavelet $s(t)$ contains information on the time history of the earthquake and the propagation effects owing to the structure of the Earth from the source to the base of the crust.
elements.sent-2411-495: The generation of radial receiver function involves the deconvolution of the radial component with the vertical component to predominantly extract SV converted waves.
elements.sent-2411-496: As shown in Appendix~\ref{sec:appn1 , the radial receiver function can be written as \begin{equation}r(t,\p)= \int_{\tau}s_{\text{a}}(t-\tau,\p)\grz(\tau,\p)\,\text{d}\tau + \nt(t,\p), \label{eqn:rfn}\end{equation} where $s_{\text{a }$ denotes a zero-phase signal convolved with $\grz$, which depicts the cross-correlation between $\gr$ and $\gz$.}}
elements.sent-2411-497:
The nuisance effects are due to 1) $s_{	ext{a }$ which blurs $\grz$, and 2) the additive term $
t$ which arises from the cross-correlation between seismic noise ($
r$ and $
z$) and the crustal impulse responses ($\gz$ and $\gr$).}
elements.sent-2411-498: The purpose of this paper is to extract crustal effects in $\grz$, while minimizing nuisance effects for both radial and transverse RF.
elements.sent-2411-499: Let us examine how $r$ changes with the wavenumber vector $\p$ in Eq.~\ref{eqn:rfn .}
elements.sent-2411-500: These changes can be due to the functions $\grz$, $s_\text{a}$, or $\nt$.
elements.sent-2411-501: We assume that the function $\grz$ changes smoothly with $\p$, in contrast to $s_{\text{a }$ and $\nt$, which fluctuate rapidly since each $\p$ corresponds to a distinct earthquake with a unique source time function and seismic noise.}
elements.sent-2411-502: This allowed us to distinguish the smooth variations of $\grz$ attributed to the geometry and anisotropy of the crustal layers from the rapid fluctuations of nuisance terms $s_{\text{a }$ and $\nt$.}
elements.sent-2411-503: Our assumption essentially means, when analyzing the RFs of teleseismic earthquakes situated closely together, the crustal effects of $\grz$ are coherent across RFs as $s_\text{a}$ is a zero-phase signal.
elements.sent-2411-504: A numerical validation of this assumption is detailed in Appendix.~\ref{sec:coherency.}

elements.subsection-2411-016: Multiple stations and datapoints for training
elements.sent-2411-515: Our network is trained using datapoints from multiple stations simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-516: Thus, the total number of datapoints is the aggregate of all bins across all the stations.
elements.sent-2411-517: Our main objective is to detect subtle variations in crustal effects between different datapoints, while minimizing the nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-518: The training dataset is the collection of all radial and transverse datapoints: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:traindata} \mathbf{T} = \{\rec_{j}\,\mid 1 \leq j \leq \Nn_b\} \cup \{\mathbf{t}_{j}\,\mid 1 \leq j \leq \Nn_b\},\end{equation} where $\rec_j$ and $\mathbf{t _j$ denotes the radial and transverse datapoint associated with the $j$th bin, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-519: Each radial RF datapoint in the training set is given by the vector \begin{equation}\label{eq:datapoint} \rec_j= [\rec_j^{1}, \rec_j^{2}, \ldots, \rec_j^{\Nn_j}],\end{equation} where we collect all RFs in a specific backazimuth epicentral distance bin associated with a particular station.
elements.sent-2411-520: The $\mathbf{r _j$ is reshaped into a matrix with dimensions $\Nn_t \times \Nn_j$ for training input.}
elements.sent-2411-521: Note that crustal effects are coherent across all RFs in a data point $\rec_j$.
elements.sent-2411-522: Moreover, this construction of datapoints can be extended to RFs resulting from different seismic phases, such as S, SKS, and PKP, which is reserved for future study.

The decoder performs forward modeling to generate RFs using the sampled latent codes from the posterior. } \label{fig:network}
elements.sent-2411-523: We will now focus on training the symmetric variational autoencoder (SymVAE) \citep{bharadwaj2024vsymae using the datapoints constructed in the previous section.}
elements.sent-2411-524: Appendix \ref{sec:vae provides a detailed review of standard variational autoencoders~\citep{Kingma2014,bishop2023deep,prince2023understanding --- we will expand on that discussion here.}}
elements.sent-2411-525: The main advantage of SymVAE over the standard VAE lies in its ability to learn a disentangled latent representation of the datapoints.
elements.sent-2411-526: From the Appendix.~\ref{sec:vae ,recall that the encoder maps each datapoint to the latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-527: The encoder of SymVAE is constrained to ensure that a few dimensions of latent space uniquely capture the coherent crustal effects, while other dimensions capture the nuisance effects (see Fig.~\ref{fig:network ).}
elements.sent-2411-528: In other words, the latent space is partitioned into the following two components.
elements.sent-2411-529: This component, denoted using a vector of random variables $\coh$, captures the crustal effects on the receiver side.
elements.sent-2411-530: For a datapoint $\rec_j$ of $\mathbf{T}$, the RFs have a similar backazimuth and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-531: These RFs are associated with raypaths that pass through the same receiver-side crust.
elements.sent-2411-532: This component represents the coherent crustal effects across all the RFs.
elements.sent-2411-533: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:network , the posterior associated with this component, i.e. $\post(\coh\mid \rec_j)$, is output by the symmetric encoder network $\mathbf{h _q$.}}
elements.sent-2411-534: This component is RF specific, as it represents the nuisance effects, due to the deconvolution process and seismic noise, unique to the specified RF.
elements.sent-2411-535: It is denoted using a vector of random variables $\nuis^i$ corresponding to the $i$th RF in $\rec_j$.
elements.sent-2411-536: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:network , the posterior associated with $\nuis^i$, i.e., $\post(\nuis^i\mid\rec^i_j)$, is output by the nuisance encoder network $\mathbf{h _p$.}}
elements.sent-2411-537: When we say encoders output posterior distributions, it means they provide the parameters of these distributions, specifically a mean vector and diagonal covariance matrix for multivariate Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-538: As an example, consider the nuisance encoder that is responsible for capturing the nuisance effects associated with each RF $\rec^i_j$.
elements.sent-2411-539: The convolutional neural network $\mathbf{h _p$, detailed in Appendix~\ref{sec:training , determines the mean and diagonal covariance of the posterior distribution $\post(\nuis^i\mid\rec^i_j)$.}}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Accumulation of crustal information using symmetric encoder
elements.sent-2411-540: The primary attribute of the SymVAE symmetric encoder is its ability to accumulate crustal information from multiple available RFs.
elements.sent-2411-541: In other words, for a given datapoint $\rec_j$, each RF $\rec^i_j$ is considered an independent observation that contains information on crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-542: The symmetric encoder first generates a posterior distribution of crustal information for each RF within $\rec_j$ --- we denote the distribution associated with $i$th RF by $\post(\coh\mid\rec^i_j)$.
elements.sent-2411-543: The convolutional neural network $\mathbf{h _q$ (detailed in Appendix~\ref{sec:training ) determines the mean and diagonal covariance of the posterior $\post(\coh\mid\rec^i_j)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-544: The encoder then combines all these posterior distributions using the principle of conjunction of information states as indicated by ~\citep{Tarantola2005 , where the accumulated crustal information is represented through the posterior \begin{equation}\post(\coh\mid \rec_j) = \post(\coh\mid \rec^{1}_j)\, \wedge \, \post(\coh\mid \rec^{2}_j)\, \wedge \cdots \varpropto \prod_{i} \, \post(\coh\mid \rec^{i}_j).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-545: Here, $\wedge$ denotes the conjunction, which means that as more independent information on crustal effects is collected through additional RFs within a datapoint, the precision of the crustal effects is enhanced.
elements.sent-2411-546: The conjunction limits the capture of nuisance effects, specific to individual RFs, aiding in the disentanglement of crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-547: Note that the accumulated information remains unchanged regardless of the order of the RFs within a datapoint, meaning that it is symmetric with respect to their ordering.
elements.sent-2411-548: As SymVAE models $\post(\coh\mid\rec^i_j)$ for each $i$ as a multivariate Gaussian with diagonal covariance, $\post(\coh\mid \rec_j,\theta_\qln)$ is also a multivariate Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-549: The generation of RFs from the latent space is performed by a convolutional decoder network $\mathbf{f}$ (details in Appendix~\ref{sec:training ).}
elements.sent-2411-550: The decoder needs a latent code as input, which is constructed by drawing samples from the posterior distributions.
elements.sent-2411-551: As shown in the Fig~\ref{fig:network , the coherent code sampled from the posterior $\post(\coh\mid\rec_j)$ is labeled $\hat{\coh _j$, and the nuisance code sampled from $\post(\nuis^i\mid\rec^i_j)$ is labeled as $\hat{\nuis ^i_j$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-552: The RF generated by the decoder will contain features associated with these latent codes --- the coherent code sampled from $\post(\coh\mid\rec_j)$ represents the crustal effects of the datapoint $\rec_j$, while the nuisance code from $\post(\nuis^i\mid\rec^i_j)$ represents unique nuisance effects linked to the RF $\rec^i_j$.
elements.sent-2411-553: The decoder $\mathbf{f}$ provides the mean of the likelihood distribution $\likl(\rec^i_j\mid\hat{\coh _j,\hat{\nuis ^i_j)$, a multivariate Gaussian with constant variance for all variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-554: The mean of the likelihood distribution $\likl(\rec^i_j\mid\hat{\coh _j,\hat{\nuis ^i_j)$ corresponds to the generated RF linked to the latent codes $\hat{\coh _j$ and $\hat{\nuis ^i_j$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-555: As described in Appendix~\ref{sec:vae , optimizing encoder and decoder parameters to maximize the evidence lower bound will not only ensure accurate reproduction of the RFs in the training dataset $\mathbf{T}$ After training SymVAE, the code pertaining to the coherent crustal effects of the datapoint $\rec_j$ is given by the mean of the accumulated posterior $\post(\coh\mid\rec_j)$, which is denoted as $\mcoh_j$.}

elements.sent-2411-709: Our method has two distinct features: 1) it learns the distribution of nuisance effects without assuming a predefined distribution; 2) by training multiple RFs simultaneously and reducing nuisance effects with a unified network, it does not process each RF independently, compared to most of the existing methods.

elements.sent-2411-716: Training SymVAE, similar to any deep neural network, necessitates hyperparameter tuning, focusing on coherent and nuisance code lengths.
elements.sent-2411-717: Selecting these optimally aids effectively disentangling nuisance from crustal effects and boosts generative performance.
elements.sent-2411-718: In our case, hyperparameter tuning is carried out through synthetic experiments.
elements.sent-2411-719: This research assumes that the RFs from nearby earthquakes share coherent crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-720: Coherency implies minimal traveltime differences for converted arrivals from such earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-721: What characterizes minimal and nearby? For a specified maximum frequency of interest, the response depends on the depth of the structure being examined on the receiver's side.
elements.sent-2411-722: For example, to successfully extract converted seismic waves from the crust or upper mantle, it is possible to group relatively distant earthquakes.
elements.sent-2411-723: As travel time differences increase for deeper mantle structures, the earthquakes must be nearer.
elements.sent-2411-724: We introduce an innovative approach to removing these unwanted nuisance effects from RFs through an unsupervised deep learning framework using variational symmetric autoencoders.
elements.sent-2411-725: This method effectively disentangled the nuisance effects from crustal effects in the latent space, allowing the generation of virtual RFs with minimal nuisance effects by optimizing in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-726: Our approach outperforms linear averaging and phase-weighted averaging in denoising synthetic RFs, which are affected by realistic non-Gaussian nuisance effects, resulting in high-quality and consistent RFs.

elements.sent-2411-733: We express the measured seismograms in frequency domain as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Dz(\omega,\p) = S(\omega)\Gz(\omega,\p)+\Nz(\omega) \quad \text{and} \Dr(\omega,\p) = S(\omega)\Gr(\omega,\p)+\Nr(\omega), \label{eqn:A2} \end{split}\end{equation} where $\omega$ is the angular frequency.
elements.sent-2411-734: Then, the radial receiver function is given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:A3} \mathit{R}(\omega,\p) = \frac{\Dr(\omega,\p)\Dz(\omega,\p)^{*}}{|\Dz(\omega,\p)|^2},\end{equation} where the complex conjugate of the vertical component is $\Dz(\omega,\p)^{*}$.
elements.sent-2411-735: It is important to recognize that the spectrum of $\Dz$ may contain zeros or very small values, causing instability.
elements.sent-2411-736: We represent the regularization factor by $\lambda$, which varies according to the chosen regularization method, resulting in \begin{equation}\mathit{R}(\omega,\p) = \lambda(\omega,\p)\Dr(\omega,\p)\Dz(\omega,\p)^{*}. \label{eqn:A4}\end{equation} For example, in the case of water-level regularization ~\citep{clayton1976source , \begin{equation}\lambda(\omega,\p) = \frac{W(\omega)}{\text{max}\big(|\Dz(\omega,\p)|^{2}, c\;\underset{\forall\,\omega}{\text{max}}\,(|\Dz(\omega,\p)|^{2})\big)},\end{equation} where $c$ is the water-level parameter and $W(\omega)= \text{exp}\,(-\frac{\omega {4a^{2 )$ is a Gaussian filter with a width given by the parameter $a$.}}}}

elements.section-2411-016: Variational Autoencoder: A Deep Generative Model
elements.sent-2411-741: Here, we will review variational autoencoder (VAE) and its training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-742: We will clarify how VAE learns a compressed probabilistic data representation and generates new datapoints from the training data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-743: Following this, we introduce SymVAE, as an extension of standard VAE, designed to improve the interpretation of generated virtual data.
elements.sent-2411-744: Note that, in this paper, we used SymVAE to generate virtual receiver functions with minimal nuisance effects.
elements.sent-2411-745: VAE is a latent variable model, which means it learns to represent the training data using latent (or) hidden variables.
elements.sent-2411-746: We represent the $i$th datapoint in the training set $\mathbf{X}$ as $\data_i$, and the vector of latent variables using $\lat$.
elements.sent-2411-747: Generally, $\lat$ has lower dimensions compared to the datapoint $\data_i$.
elements.sent-2411-748: Using a geophysical analogy, latent variables can be thought of as the model parameters we estimate while solving inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-749: VAE inverts each datapoint, i.e. each element of the training dataset, independently.
elements.sent-2411-750: This means that each datapoint in the training dataset has a specific latent-variable setting associated with it.
elements.sent-2411-751: In traditional geophysical inverse modeling, we use the physics of the problem at hand to derive the posterior distribution associated with the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-752: In the case of VAEs, we perform variational inference, which means, we train a neural network $\mathbf{h}$ to output the posterior distribution $Q(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta)$ associated with a datapoint.
elements.sent-2411-753: This network $\mathbf{h}$ with parameters $\theta$ that outputs the posterior for each datapoint is known as the encoder network.
elements.sent-2411-754: The posterior is modeled as a multivariate normal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-755: After performing inverse modeling using the encoder network, we employ another network $\mathbf{f}$ to perform the forward modeling step.
elements.sent-2411-756: Specifically, while the encoder maps each datapoint to its latent variable representation (that is, solving an inverse problem), the decoder $\mathbf{f}$ takes a sample latent code and generates the corresponding datapoint (that is, solving a forward problem).
elements.sent-2411-757: The decoder network with parameters $\phi$ outputs the mean of the likelihood distribution $\likl(\data_i\mid\lat,\phi)$ as $\mathbf{f (\hat{\lat ,\phi)$, where $\hat{\lat}$ denotes a sample latent code.}}
elements.sent-2411-758: The likelihood is modeled as a multivariate normal distribution with a fixed diagonal covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-759: In this way, the encoder–decoder pair establishes a probabilistic mapping between the data space and the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-760: We present a concise derivation of the loss function for learning the encoder and decoder parameters in a VAE.
elements.sent-2411-761: Following \cite{bishop2023deep , we assume an independently and identically distributed training dataset $\mathbf{X =\{\data_1,\data_2,\cdots\}$, and start with an optimization of decoder parameters as \begin{eqnarray}\label{eqn:log-opt} \tilde{\phi} = \arg \max_\phi \,\log\left(\likl(\mathbf{X}\mid\phi)\right) \nonumber = \arg \max_\phi \,\sum_i \log\left(\likl(\data_i\mid\phi)\right) \nonumber = \arg \max_\phi \left[ \sum_i {\int \post_i(\lat) \log\left(\frac{\likl(\data_i\mid\lat,\phi)\likl(\lat)}{\post_i(\lat)}\right) \text{d}\lat} + \sum_i D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left({\post_i(\lat)}||{P(\lat\mid\data_i,\phi)}\right)\right],\end{eqnarray} where $\post_i(\lat)$ is an arbitrary distribution of the latent variable and $D_{\mathrm{KL (\post_i(\lat)||\likl(\lat\mid\data_i,\phi))$ represents the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the arbitrary distribution $\post_i(\lat)$ and the posterior $\likl(\lat\mid\data_i,\phi)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-762: As the output of the encoder $\mathbf{h}$ approximates the posterior distribution, we substitute $\post_i(\lat)$ with $\post(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta)$ in the above equation and ignore the calculation of the KL term, which is intractable.
elements.sent-2411-763: In other words, when the encoder output $Q(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta)$ successfully approximates the true posterior $P(\lat\mid\data_i,\phi)$, the KL term approaches zero.
elements.sent-2411-764: Subsequent to this substitution, we aim to optimize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) to derive the encoder and decoder parameters as \begin{eqnarray}\label{eqn:log-opt} \tilde{\phi},~\tilde{\theta} = \arg \max_{\phi, \theta} \sum_i {\int \post(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta) \log\left(\frac{\likl(\data_i\mid\lat,\phi)\likl(\lat)}{\post(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta)}\right) \text{d}\lat}, \nonumber = \arg \max_\phi \left[ \sum_i \int \post(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta) \log\left(\likl(\data\mid\lat,\phi)\right) \mathrm{d}\lat - \sum_i D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(\post(\lat\mid\data_i,\theta)||\likl(\lat)\right)\right].\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-765: Here, the prior distribution $P(\lat)$ is simply modeled as a multivariate normal distribution with zero mean and identity covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-766: Finally, after training VAE, we can select a sample $\hat{\lat}$ from the latent space, referred to in this article as latent code, and generate a new datapoint using the decoder as $\mathbf{f (\hat{\lat ,\phi)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-767: In the context of SymVAE, each datapoint $\data_i$ comprises of a group of receiver function (shown in Eq.~\ref{eqn:traindata ).}
elements.sent-2411-768: The latent variable $\lat$ is structured to include $\coh$ and $\nuis^i$ corresponding to the $i$th receiver function in the datapoint.
elements.sent-2411-769: This structuring of the latent space allows for an interpretable generation of new receiver functions.
elements.subsection-2411-023: Evaluation of quality in synthetic experiments
elements.sent-2411-770: In the synthetic experiments, the quality of nuisance-minimized RF is determined by comparing it with the true RF.
elements.sent-2411-771: The similarity between the two is quantified by calculating the normalized correlation coefficient (NCC) between the nuisance-minimized RF, $\drec$, and the true RF, $\rrec$, at each data point $\rec_j$ as follows \begin{equation}\ncc(\drec,\rrec)= \frac{{(\drec)}^\mathrm{T} \rrec}{||\drec||_{2}\,\, ||\rrec||_2},\end{equation} where $||\drec ||_2$ and $||\rrec ||_2$ denotes the $\ell_2$ norm of $\drec$ and $\rrec$ respectively.
elements.subsection-2411-024: Evaluation of quality in real data application
elements.sent-2411-772: In real data applications, since there is no true RF, we calculate the mean normalized correlation coefficient (MNCC) of nuisance-minimized RF with the raw RFs in a datapoint.
elements.sent-2411-773: For instance, the MNCC of the optimized virtual RF $\vir^{\text{opt _j$, related to the datapoint $\rec_j$, is given by \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ncc} \ncc_{\text{m}}(\vir^{\text{opt}}_j,\rec_j)=\frac{1}{n_j}\sum^{n_j}_{i} \ncc\,(\vir^{\text{opt}}_j,\rec^{i}_j),\end{equation} where $n_j$ denoted the number of RFs in the datapoint $\rec_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-774: The MNCC $\ncc_{\text{m }$ quantifies how well the optimized virtual RF correlates with the observed RFs in $\rec_j$.}
elements.sent-2411-775: A higher $\ncc_{\text{m }$ indicates more effective information extraction from the observed data.}
elements.sent-2411-776: However, calculating the MNCC $\ncc_{\text{m }$ for the linearly averaged RF using Eq.~\ref{eqn:ncc can be misleading, as self-correlation of one RF within the datapoint $\rec_j$ can skew the result.}}
elements.sent-2411-777: To address this, we excluded the identical RF from the averaging process in all NCC calculations within a datapoint.
elements.sent-2411-779: Note that this custom averaging of RFs is only employed while calculating the MNCC.
elements.sent-2411-780: Furthermore, the P-wave at $t=0$ can significantly affect the correlation coefficient in most RFs, reducing the sensitivity to crustal effects.
elements.sent-2411-781: To address this, we calculate the NCC using time samples from $t = 2.5$ s to the end.
elements.sent-2411-782: In this section, we detail the network architecture used for training on the RFs dataset and provide a short overview of the training process.
elements.sent-2411-783: In our implementation of the convolutional encoder-decoder architecture, we designed the convolutional layers to extract and reconstruct key features from one-dimensional input RFs.
elements.sent-2411-784: The design of the convolutional layers takes inspiration from AlexNet~\citep{NIPS2012_c399862d .}
elements.sent-2411-785: In both encoders, we employed a sequence of five 1D convolutional layers with the following configuration: Conv(50, 1→5) - LeakyReLU - Conv(20, 5→10) - LeakyReLU - MeanPool(4) - Conv(5, 10→50) - LeakyReLU - Conv(5, 50→40) - LeakyReLU - MeanPool(4).
elements.sent-2411-786: After the last MeanPool, we apply a flattening operation followed by a dense layer that maps the resulting feature vector to the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-787: In decoder, two dense layers are used to transform the concatenated latent code back to the original signal shape.
elements.sent-2411-788: The resulting signal is then processed through five 1D convolutional layers: Conv(50, 1→40) - LeakyReLU - Conv(20, 40→10) - LeakyReLU - Conv(20, 10→10) - LeakyReLU - Conv(5, 10→5) - LeakyReLU - Conv(5, 5→1) - LeakyReLU.
elements.sent-2411-789: Datapoint refers to a set of RFs with similar backazimuth angle and epicentral distance.
elements.sent-2411-790: Training dataset consists of all datapoints across stations.
elements.sent-2411-791: For both synthetic and real data, each datapoint was partitioned so that 80\% of the RFs were used for training, while the remaining 20\% were reserved for testing.
elements.sent-2411-792: The testing dataset is not involved in the network training, instead, it is utilized to assess the network's capability to generalize to new, unseen RFs.
elements.sent-2411-793: This evaluation is critical to ensure that the network does not overfit the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-794: The training dataset for the radial and transverse components was trained on SymVAE network independently.
elements.sent-2411-795: The coherent and nuisance code lengths are crucial for SymVAE to extract significant features from RFs and reconstruct them accurately.
elements.sent-2411-796: The reconstruction loss curves for both training and testing datasets guide the determination of the optimal latent code lengths.
elements.sent-2411-797: For synthetic RFs, the coherent code length ($q$) was set to 50, while the nuisance code length ($p$) was set to 20.
elements.sent-2411-798: For real RF data, these lengths were set to 100 and 20, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-799: The training of the SymVAE network was stopped at 190 epochs, where the reconstruction loss of both the training and testing dataset converged.
elements.sent-2411-800: The learning rate was reduced in three stages: 0.0004, 0.0002, and 0.0001.
elements.sent-2411-801: The training time of SymVAE for both synthetic and real training datasets is around 15 min.
elements.sent-2411-802: NVIDIA GeForce 3090 (24 GB) GPU card was used in the training.
